frontmatter.title: Restriction of path objects along quasi-inverses

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object and let B be a type. Given a quasi-inverse function g \colon B \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {}, we can restrict the path object \mathcal {A} to a path object structure g^* \mathcal {A} on B as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert g^* \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv B \\ x \approx _{ g^* \mathcal {A} } y &: \equiv gx \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } gy \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ g^* \mathcal {A} } x &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } \mathopen {} \left ( gx \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

mainmatter.p: Fixing x:B, we must show that \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } gx \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } gy is a proposition. But this is a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } gx \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y, which is a proposition by the univalence of \mathcal {A}; thus the result follows from the closure of propositions under retracts ().
If B is a retract of A and A is a proposition, then B is a proposition.
This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.
When s \colon A \to B is a section of r \colon B \to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.

A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for all x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y is a proposition, or (equivalently), is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \right ) \mathclose {}. We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

A quasi-inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is a function g \colon B \to A equipped with homotopies \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{ B } and \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A }, rendering g simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
Although no coherence laws are assumed for \eta and \epsilon, we shall refer to them respectively as the unit and counit of the quasi-inverse.
